column1: camptothecin,  are  also  derived  from  plants1,2.  Among  the  wide  diversity  of  plants,  Fagonia  attracted  great
attention due to their diverse pharmacological activities such as anti-allergic, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory,
blood  purification,  and  anti-cancer  activities3–5.  The  Fagonia  genus  belongs  to  the  family  Zygophyllaceae,
having 35 species, including Fagonia Indica, Fagonia Cretica, Fagonia Arabica, and Fagonia Laevis, etc., widely
distributed in Egypt, Western India, Afghanistan, and Pakistan6. Among all the above species, Fagonia indica
gained  global  interest  due  to  its  variety  of  active  phytochemical  constituents,  including  tannins,  flavonoids,
sterols,  coumarins,  alkaloids,  triterpenoids,  saponins,  glycosides,  and  pectin7,8.  Fagonia  indica  has  been  used
in traditional medicines for various diseases because of its antipyretic, antidiabetic, antioxidant, antimicrobial,
laxative, anti-inflammatory, and anti-cancer properties9.

column2: The morbidity and mortality rate associated with cancer is very high, and it is the second foremost cause

column1: of death worldwide, with approximately 9.7 million deaths reported in 2022. By 2050, the cancer burden will
increase by approximately 77%10–12. Several conventional therapeutic modalities, such as surgery, radiotherapy,
chemotherapy,  hormonal  therapy,  and  immunotherapy,  are  available  for  cancer  treatment13.  However,  two
important factors associated with them are drug toxicity and drug resistance, which are major hurdles towards
effective treatment. Thus, new approaches are required to effectively minimize these limitations and improve the
quality of life of cancer patients14. In this context, the anti-cancer therapeutic effect of fagonia indica has been
investigated. However, the therapeutic use of this plant in traditional medicine on a scientific basis has not been
systematically examined. Some scientific investigations have revealed the anticancer potential of this plant15.

column2: Published research reported the cell death induced by fagonia indica extract in multidrug-resistant breast

column1: (MDR) and colon cancer by down-regulating the expression of ICAM-1, VEGF, and NF-kB. It inhibits cell cycle
regulation and triggers apoptosis by activating p53 and FOXO3a in breast cancer16. This plant extract comprises
phytochemicals in their glycosidic forms, whose aglycones function as anti-cancer agents against breast cancer17.
The  steroidal  saponin  glycoside  extracted  from  Fagonia  indica  causes  cell  death  via  necrosis  or  apoptosis  in
breast (MDA-MB-468, MCF-7) and colon cancer (Caco-2) cells18.

column2: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a promising treatment for various types of cancer and selectively targets

column1: cancer cells. Phytochemicals in plant extracts have been shown to enhance PDT efficacy by increasing reactive
oxygen species production and initiating apoptosis in cancer cells. PDT effects in Aloe vera’s secondary metabolites
were found through both Type-I and Type-II reactions. Numerous studies have explored the potential of plant
extracts to enhance PDT efficacy attributed to naturally occurring photosensitive compounds. Phytocompounds
offer  advantages  over  photosensitizers  and  conventional  therapies,  including  their  broad  therapeutic  effects,
safety, and affordability19.

column2: Fagonia  indica  extract  is  a  viable  candidate  for  cancer  treatment;  however,  it  contains  a  complex  mixture

column1: of  phytochemicals
column4: its  solubility,  permeability,  and  bioavailability20.  These  can  affect

column1: absorption and delivery of the active compounds to the targeted area. To overcome these limitations, a suitable
nanoencapsulation is required to enhance its bioavailability and solubility, thereby improving cellular uptake.
FDA-approved Polymeric (PLGA) nanocarriers have been extensively used in nano-drug delivery systems due
to their excellent biocompatibility, non-toxicity, controlled drug release, and biodegradability21.

column2: Previous literature also validated that plant extracts have been successfully encapsulated in PLGA NPs to

column1: improve  their  therapeutic  efficacy.  Ribeiro  and  coworkers  have  explored  the  anticancer  activity  of  Uncaria
tomentosa extract-loaded PLGA and PCL NPs against prostate cancer (DU145 and LNCaP) cell lines22. Similarly,

column2: Roque  et  al.  demonstrated  that  Glycyrrhiza  glabra  extract  loaded  PLGA,  PLA,  and  alginate  NPs  to  improve
bio-adhesion and effectiveness in treating oral yeast infections23. Moreover, berberine and Callistemon citrinus
extract-loaded  PLGA  nano-formulations  showed  better  cytotoxicity  effects  against  breast  cancer  (MCF-10A,
MCF-7, and MDA-MB 231) cell lines than free drugs. These investigations highlight the potential of nanocarrier-
based drug delivery systems to improve the efficacy of plant’s bioactive compounds24. Previous studies explored
nano-formulated Fagonia cretica extract to enhance cellular uptake and cytotoxicity against HUH-7, MCF-7,

column1: HCEC, and Hep-2 cell lines20.

column2: The present study aims to encapsulate the hydrophobic extract of FI into PLGA nanoparticles to enhance the
solubility and bioavailability of FI extract. Synthesized FI NPs were characterized using UV–vis, fluorescence,
Fourier  transform  infrared  (FTIR),  and  X-ray  diffraction  (XRD)  spectroscopy.  The  encapsulation  and  drug
loading  efficiency  of  synthesized  NPs  were  systematically  evaluated.  The  in-vitro  cytotoxic  and  phototoxic
studies were performed against a breast cancer cell line (BT-474).

Collection and preparation of plant extract
The Fagonia indica plant was collected from the village of Khushab, Pakistan. The aerial part of the plant was
washed three times with distilled water and shade-dried at room temperature. After drying, it was crushed into
a fine powder using an electric grinder and kept in an airtight bottle for future processing. To prepare the plant
extract, 30 g of powdered sample of FI were subjected to extraction using 300 ml of 100% ethanol (1:10) for
72 h. The mixture was stirred with a sterilized glass rod every 24 h and filtered using Whatman filter paper. This
filtrate was then concentrated using a SENCO® rotary evaporator and dried at 50 °C in an oven. The FI extract
was weighed and kept at 4 ◦C in glass vials for further use. On the day of the experiment, a series of working
